topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, a flimsy piece of glossy paper featuring a sprawling desert landscape, lay crumpled on my desk. My thumb traced the faded blue ink of the destination address, and a cold knot tightened in my stomach. Liam was moving. Across the country. I stared at the framed photo of us laughing on the porch swing, the sunlight in the picture feeling suddenly brittle. Every time I tried to focus on the mundane task of sorting mail, my attention snagged on the reality of his departure. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as if I were holding it too tightly. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my living room, the floorboards protesting under my hurried steps. I needed to call him, to ask if this was serious, but my throat felt constricted, the words refusing to form into anything coherent. The silence in the apartment amplified the drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I kept re-reading the confirmation email, the font blurring slightly under the pressure of my gaze. New state, new life, a thousand miles separating us. My hands began to tremble, the edges of the screen blurring into streaks of light. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to catch a glimpse of the familiar street, feeling a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt. The air felt too thick to draw in. I pressed my palms against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself, but my muscles felt tight, coiled. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but only a small, pathetic sound escaped my lips. I turned away from the window, sinking onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands. The world seemed to tilt slightly, the edges of my vision darkening as a wave of profound unease washed over me.

[story 3]

When Sarah dropped the news, the sound of her voice was startlingly bright, almost too cheerful for the gravity of the situation. "I got the lease! I'm moving to Seattle!" The words hung in the air, heavy and undeniable. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a sensation like being underwater. I managed a tight, strained smile, the muscles in my jaw locking up. I nodded, trying to process the shift in the landscape of our friendship, but my mind felt like static, buzzing uncontrollably. I started fiddling with the loose thread on my sweater, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. Each second stretched into an eternity. I kept glancing at the clock, the ticking of the wall clock sounding deafeningly loud, marking time that felt impossibly slow, dragging toward an unknown horizon.
